1: "When Israel came out of Egypt, when the family of Jacob left a people of foreign speech" — the Exodus as the paradigmatic act of divine redemption. First Corinthians Psalms 10:1-11 explicitly interprets the Exodus typologically: the events in the wilderness "were written down as warnings for us, on whom the culmination of the ages has come." Christ enacts the definitive new Exodus — Luke 9:31 says Moses and Elijah spoke at the Transfiguration about "his departure (exodon), which he was about to bring to fulfillment at Jerusalem." The Exodus of Psalms 114:1 is the prototype; Christ's death and resurrection is the antitype.
2: "Judah became God's holy place, Israel his kingdom" — the Exodus establishing YHWH's sanctuary and kingdom. First Peter Psalms 2:9: "But you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession." The community born from the Exodus becomes God's sanctuary and kingdom — and the community born from the new Exodus (Christ's death and resurrection) is the new Israel, God's holy dwelling in the Spirit (Ephesians 2:21-22: "In him the whole building is joined together and rises to become a holy temple in the Lord").
3: "The sea saw and fled; the Jordan reversed its course" — the cosmic response to YHWH's presence: the Red Sea parts, the Jordan stops. Jesus' baptism in the Jordan (Matthew 3:13-17) re-enacts Israel's crossing — he goes through the waters as the true Israel, emerges to receive the Spirit's descent and the Father's voice: "This is my beloved Son." The Jordan reversal of Psalms 114:3 is re-enacted in the baptism of Christ who goes through the waters as Israel's head and comes out as the acknowledged Son.
4: "The mountains leaped like rams and the hills like young lambs" — the Sinai theophany causes creation to convulse. This cosmic responsiveness to the divine presence accompanies Christ's key moments: his birth (the star of Matthew 2:2), his death ("the earth shook and the rocks split," Matthew 27:51), his resurrection ("There was a violent earthquake," Matthew 28:2), and his return ("the heavenly bodies will be shaken," Matthew 24:29). The mountains leaping at the Sinai theophany are the type of creation's response to the presence of the incarnate Son.
5: "What happened to you, sea, that you ran? And you, Jordan — that you reversed your course?" — the psalmist interrogates the creation, demanding an account of its dramatic response. The implicit answer is: YHWH was there. At Christ's baptism, the heavens "were opened" (Matthew 3:16) — creation responds to the arrival of the one whose presence is greater than the theophany at Sinai. The interrogation of the sea and Jordan makes the point that only the divine presence explains such events; and Christ's presence provokes equal signs throughout the Gospels.
6: "You mountains — why did you leap like rams? You hills, like young lambs?" — the rhetorical question repeated. Hebrews 12:18-24 contrasts the terrifying Sinai theophany with the new covenant's "Mount Zion, the city of the living God... Jesus the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel." The mountains that leaped at Sinai are surpassed by the eschatological Mount Zion to which the new covenant community comes through Christ — the greater theophany in a better Mediator.
7: "Tremble before the Lord, O earth — tremble before the God of Jacob" — the call for creation to tremble before YHWH. Hebrews 12:26-27: "At that time his voice shook the earth, but now he has promised, 'Once more I will shake not only the earth but also the heavens.' The words 'once more' indicate the removing of what can be shaken — that is, created things — so that what cannot be shaken may remain." The earth-trembling at the Exodus theophany is the prototype of the eschatological cosmic shaking that comes through Christ — the final trembling before the Lord who comes in judgment.
8: "who turns solid rock into a pool of water and hard flint into a flowing spring" — the Meribah miracle: water from the rock (Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:11). First Corinthians Psalms 10:4: "they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ." The water from the rock in Psalms 114:8 receives among the most direct Christological identifications in the entire NT — Paul says the preexistent Christ was the rock. John 7:37-38: "Let anyone who is thirsty come to me and drink. Whoever believes in me, as Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from within them." The hard flint turned to flowing spring is Christ himself, poured out through the Spirit.
